questions.question: What equitable principle is encapsulated in the phrase 'a person cannot approbate and reprobate'?

options.A: A person cannot be both a trustee and a beneficiary of the same trust
options.B: A person cannot accept a benefit under an instrument while simultaneously rejecting an obligation imposed by the same instrument
options.C: A person cannot enter into a contract that is both beneficial and onerous without taking legal advice
options.D: A person cannot make a will that benefits one family member at the expense of another

questions.explanation: The maxim 'a person cannot approbate and reprobate' is the foundation of the doctrine of election. It means that a person cannot accept a benefit conferred by an instrument (such as a will) while simultaneously repudiating or rejecting an obligation imposed by the same instrument. The doctrine requires consistency — a person who benefits from an instrument must accept the consequences and obligations it imposes.

questions.question: What is the classic formulation of the doctrine of election in the testamentary context?

options.A: A person who makes a will must elect which beneficiaries are to benefit from the estate
options.B: A beneficiary who takes a benefit under a will must also submit to any burden imposed by the same will, even if that burden affects property belonging to the beneficiary
options.C: A testator must elect between leaving their property to family members or to charity
options.D: A personal representative must elect which creditors of the estate are to be paid first

questions.explanation: The classic formulation of the doctrine of election is that a person who takes a benefit under a will must also submit to any burden imposed by the same will. Crucially, this applies even where the burden affects property belonging to the beneficiary themselves — if the testator purports to dispose of A's own property in the will, and also gives A a benefit, A must elect between accepting the benefit (and giving up their property as the will directs) or rejecting the benefit (and keeping their own property, but compensating the disappointed beneficiary).

questions.question: In Cooper v Cooper [1874], the House of Lords explained the operation of the doctrine of election. What did the court hold?

options.A: That election only arises where both parties to the election are named as beneficiaries in the will
options.B: That where a testator gives property belonging to A to B, and also gives a benefit to A, A must elect — either accept the benefit and conform to the will, or reject the benefit and retain their own property
options.C: That a testator has the power in equity to dispose of any property regardless of ownership, provided all parties are given notice
options.D: That the doctrine of election does not apply where the testator was unaware that the property belonged to the beneficiary

questions.explanation: In Cooper v Cooper [1874], the House of Lords held that where a testator purports to give property belonging to A to B, and also gives a benefit to A in the same will, A faces a choice: (1) elect to take the benefit, in which case A must give up their own property as directed by the will and allow it to pass to B; or (2) elect against the will, retaining their own property but forfeiting the benefit — and compensating B out of what would have been A's benefit under the will. The doctrine prevents A from taking the benefit while also keeping their own property.

questions.question: Where a person elects AGAINST the will (elects to retain their own property), what is the consequence for the disappointed beneficiary?

options.A: The disappointed beneficiary receives nothing and has no claim against the estate
options.B: The elector must compensate the disappointed beneficiary out of the benefit the elector would have received under the will
options.C: The disappointed beneficiary can claim the full value of the property from the estate regardless of the elector's decision
options.D: The will is partially voided and the property is divided equally between all beneficiaries

questions.explanation: Where a person elects against the will — choosing to retain their own property rather than conform to the will's direction — equity requires them to compensate the disappointed beneficiary. This compensation is taken from the benefit that the elector would have received under the will. If the benefit under the will is equal to or greater than the value of the property retained, the disappointed beneficiary can be fully compensated. If the benefit is less than the value of the retained property, the disappointed beneficiary is compensated to the extent of the benefit forfeited.

questions.question: Section 55 of the Succession Act 1965 is relevant to the doctrine of election in Irish law. What does it address?

options.A: The doctrine of election in the context of wills, providing a statutory framework for how the doctrine operates in Ireland
options.B: The rectification of wills where the testator made a mistake in expressing their intentions
options.C: The order of priority for payment of debts from a deceased's estate
options.D: The conditions under which a beneficiary may disclaim a testamentary gift

questions.explanation: Section 55 of the Succession Act 1965 governs the doctrine of election in the context of wills in Irish law. It provides the statutory framework for the operation of the doctrine, maintaining the basic principle that a beneficiary must elect between the benefit conferred by the will and the property they would be required to give up. The provision reflects the general equitable principle that a person cannot approbate and reprobate under the same instrument.

questions.question: What condition must be satisfied for an election to be valid, as established in Re Chesham [1886]?

options.A: The election must be made by deed, witnessed by a solicitor
options.B: The person electing must know the full extent of both the benefit they are being offered and the burden they are required to bear before making their choice
options.C: The election must be filed with the Probate Office within six months of the grant of probate
options.D: The election must be made in the presence of all other beneficiaries of the will

questions.explanation: In Re Chesham [1886], it was established that for an election to be valid and binding, the person making the election must be fully informed — they must know the full extent of both the benefit being offered to them under the will and the burden or obligation they are being asked to accept. An election made in ignorance of either the full benefit or the full burden is not a genuine and informed election and will not bind the elector.

questions.question: A testator leaves their house to their daughter, having mistakenly believed the house belongs to them. In fact, the house belongs to their son. The will also leaves the son a substantial legacy. What must the son do under the doctrine of election?

options.A: The son keeps his house and also receives the legacy because the testator cannot dispose of property they do not own
options.B: The son must elect — either accept the legacy and allow the house to pass to the daughter as the will directs, or reject the legacy and keep his house, compensating the daughter out of the legacy he forgoes
options.C: The will is automatically void in its entirety because the testator purported to dispose of property they did not own
options.D: The son must commence proceedings to recover the house regardless of whether he wants the legacy

questions.explanation: This is the classic election scenario from Cooper v Cooper [1874]. The testator purports to give the son's property (the house) to the daughter, while also leaving the son a legacy. The son must elect: (1) if he accepts the legacy, he must allow the house to pass to the daughter as the will directs; or (2) if he keeps his house, he must forgo the legacy and compensate the daughter out of that benefit. He cannot take both the legacy and keep his house — that would be approbating and reprobating the same will.

questions.question: What happens if the value of the benefit available to compensate the disappointed beneficiary is LESS than the value of the property the elector retains?

options.A: The disappointed beneficiary is entitled to the shortfall from the general estate
options.B: The disappointed beneficiary can only be compensated to the extent of the benefit forfeited by the elector; they cannot recover more than the value of that benefit
options.C: The will is invalid and all property is distributed on intestacy
options.D: The elector is required to pay the shortfall from their own personal assets outside the estate

questions.explanation: Where the value of the benefit forfeited by the elector is less than the value of the property they retain, the disappointed beneficiary can only be compensated to the extent of the forfeited benefit. Equity does not require the elector to pay more than they would have received under the will. The disappointed beneficiary cannot recover any shortfall from the general estate or from the elector's personal assets — their remedy is limited to the value of the benefit the elector chose to forgo.

questions.question: Is the doctrine of election confined to wills, or does it apply to other instruments?

options.A: The doctrine of election is strictly confined to wills and has no application to inter vivos instruments
options.B: The doctrine of election applies to any instrument — including deeds and settlements — under which a benefit is conferred and an obligation is imposed on the same person
options.C: The doctrine of election applies only to trusts, not to contracts or wills
options.D: In Ireland, the doctrine of election has been abolished by statute for all instruments other than wills

questions.explanation: While the doctrine of election most commonly arises in the context of wills — and is now specifically addressed in Section 55 of the Succession Act 1965 in Ireland — the doctrine is not confined to wills. It applies to any instrument under which a benefit is conferred and an inconsistent obligation is imposed on the same person. It can arise under deeds, settlements, and other instruments where the same fundamental tension between a benefit and an inconsistent burden arises.

questions.question: What is the difference between the doctrine of election and the doctrine of satisfaction?

options.A: They are the same doctrine known by different names in different contexts
options.B: Satisfaction presumes a subsequent act fulfils a prior obligation; election requires a person to choose between two inconsistent rights arising under the same instrument
options.C: Satisfaction applies to inter vivos transactions; election applies only to testamentary dispositions
options.D: Election is a common law doctrine; satisfaction is purely equitable

questions.explanation: The doctrine of satisfaction and the doctrine of election are distinct, though both are equitable doctrines relating to the interplay between benefits and obligations. Satisfaction operates where a subsequent act is presumed to fulfil a prior legal obligation — for example, a legacy presumed to satisfy a pre-existing debt. Election, by contrast, operates where one instrument confers a benefit and simultaneously imposes an obligation inconsistent with a right the beneficiary already holds, requiring the beneficiary to choose between them.

questions.question: A will leaves a pension belonging to the testator's wife to the testator's son, and also leaves the wife a substantial legacy. The pension belongs beneficially to the wife. What must the wife do?

options.A: The wife can claim both the pension and the legacy because the testator lacked authority to give away her pension
options.B: The wife must elect — either take the legacy and allow the pension to pass to the son, or keep her pension and forgo the legacy (compensating the son out of the legacy she would have received)
options.C: The entire will is voidable because the testator attempted to dispose of property he did not own
options.D: The wife must challenge the will in the Probate Court on grounds of fraud before she can take any action

questions.explanation: This is a classic election scenario. The testator has purported to give the wife's property (the pension) to the son while also leaving the wife a legacy — the same instrument confers a benefit on the wife and simultaneously requires her to give up her own property. Under the doctrine of election, the wife must choose: (1) take the legacy and allow the pension to pass to the son as directed; or (2) keep her pension and forgo the legacy, compensating the son from the legacy she would have received. The doctrine prevents her from taking both the legacy and the pension.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct FE-1 approach when a question raises the doctrine of election?

options.A: Simply advise that the person has no obligation to give up their own property as the testator could not lawfully dispose of it
options.B: Identify the instrument, identify the benefit conferred on the elector, identify the property belonging to the elector that the instrument purports to deal with, state the choice the elector faces, and advise on the consequences of each election
options.C: Advise the disappointed beneficiary to challenge the will on grounds of undue influence
options.D: Refer the matter to the Law Society of Ireland as a regulatory matter involving disposition of another's property

questions.explanation: The correct FE-1 approach to an election question is structured: (1) identify the instrument creating the obligation (usually the will); (2) identify the benefit conferred on the elector under that instrument; (3) identify the property belonging to the elector that the same instrument purports to dispose of in favour of another; (4) state the election that must be made — accept the benefit and submit to the burden, or reject the benefit and retain the property; and (5) advise on the consequences of each choice, including the obligation to compensate the disappointed beneficiary if the elector elects against the will.

questions.question: The doctrine of election requires the elector to know 'the full extent of both the benefit and the burden' (Re Chesham [1886]). What is the consequence of an election made without full knowledge?

options.A: The election is valid and binding regardless of the elector's knowledge
options.B: An election made without full knowledge of both the benefit and the burden is not a genuine election and will not bind the elector
options.C: The election is valid but can be challenged within one year of the testator's death
options.D: The elector must pay damages to the disappointed beneficiary for failing to elect promptly

questions.explanation: Under Re Chesham [1886], a valid election requires the elector to be fully informed — they must know the full extent of both what they are being offered (the benefit) and what they are being asked to give up (the burden). If the elector acts without full knowledge of either, the election is not genuine and will not bind them. This protects electors from making uninformed choices that could prejudice their rights, and reflects the equitable requirement that acts of waiver or election be made freely and with full understanding.

questions.question: Can the doctrine of election operate where the testator genuinely believed they owned the property they purported to dispose of?

options.A: No — the doctrine only applies where the testator knew the property belonged to someone else and deliberately included it in their will
options.B: Yes — the doctrine operates regardless of whether the testator knew the property belonged to another; it is concerned with the position of the beneficiary, not the testator's state of mind
options.C: No — where the testator made an honest mistake, the will is void for that item and no election arises
options.D: Yes, but only where the testator's mistake was negligent rather than genuinely innocent

questions.explanation: The doctrine of election operates regardless of whether the testator knew the property belonged to the elector. The doctrine is not concerned with the testator's state of mind or intention — it is concerned with the position of the beneficiary who has been placed in an inconsistent position by the will. Even where the testator genuinely and innocently believed they owned the property, the doctrine still requires the beneficiary to elect. The classic case of Cooper v Cooper [1874] was not premised on any fraud by the testator.

questions.question: Where the elector elects TO TAKE the benefit under the will (elects to conform to the will), what must they do with their own property?

options.A: They keep their own property — the election to take the benefit has no effect on their own property rights
options.B: They must allow their own property to pass to the beneficiary named in the will to receive it, as directed by the will
options.C: They must sell their own property and donate the proceeds to a charity of their choice
options.D: They must transfer their own property to the estate to be distributed on intestacy

questions.explanation: Where the elector chooses to take the benefit (elects to conform to the will), they must also comply with the obligation imposed by the will — specifically, they must allow their own property to pass to the beneficiary named to receive it in the will. Having elected to accept the benefit, the elector cannot simultaneously resist the burden. They affirm the entire effect of the will as it relates to them, including giving effect to the testator's purported disposition of their property.

questions.question: How does the court know what a person in the elector's position would prefer — is there a presumption about what the elector will choose?

options.A: The court presumes the elector will always conform to the will and take the benefit
options.B: Where the elector fails to make an express election, they are presumed to have elected to take the benefit under the will; the court will not infer an election against the will without evidence
options.C: Where the elector fails to elect, the will is treated as void in relation to that property
options.D: There is no presumption — the court must assess each case on its facts without any evidential starting point

questions.explanation: Where a person who must elect fails to make an express election, the general presumption is that they have elected to take the benefit under the will — that is, they are presumed to have conformed to the will. The court will not infer an election against the will (an election to retain their own property) without clear evidence of that intention. This presumption reflects the idea that most people, when faced with the choice, will prefer to accept the benefit conferred upon them rather than forgo it.

questions.question: In what sense is the doctrine of election 'equitable' rather than legal?

options.A: The doctrine operates only on equitable interests and not on legal property
options.B: The doctrine is administered only in the Commercial Court
options.C: The doctrine reflects equity's concern to prevent a person from taking advantage of an instrument in a selective and one-sided way — it requires consistency and good conscience in the assertion of rights
options.D: The doctrine can only be invoked by a beneficiary who holds an equitable rather than legal interest in property

questions.explanation: The doctrine of election is equitable because it reflects equity's fundamental concern with good conscience, consistency, and the prevention of unjust benefit. Equity will not allow a person to take the benefits of an instrument while rejecting its burdens — to do so would be unconscionable and contrary to the consistent treatment of the instrument. The doctrine gives effect to the instrument as a whole, requiring the elector to treat it consistently rather than selectively cherry-picking its advantages.

questions.question: Which of the following scenarios does NOT engage the doctrine of election?

options.A: A will gives A's own painting to B while leaving A a cash legacy
options.B: A will gives a benefit to A and simultaneously imposes a burden on A requiring them to transfer their own property to C
options.C: A will gives A a specific bequest of an item that the testator never owned and which is unconnected to any property belonging to A
options.D: A will gives A's own house to D while leaving A a life interest in the residuary estate

questions.explanation: For the doctrine of election to be engaged, the will must give a benefit to A while simultaneously purporting to dispose of property that belongs to A. If the bequest is of an item unconnected to any property owned by A — such as property belonging to a third party or the testator themselves — there is no inconsistent burden on A and no election is required. Options A, B, and D all present the classic election tension: a benefit for A combined with a purported disposition of A's own property to another.

questions.question: The doctrine of election requires that the testator must have 'intended' to dispose of the property, placing the beneficiary in the position of having to elect. Which of the following correctly states when this element is satisfied?

options.A: Only where the testator expressly acknowledges in the will that the property belongs to another
options.B: Where the will, on its proper construction, purports to dispose of property belonging to another — the testator's specific knowledge or intent is not a precondition
options.C: Only where the testator was advised by a solicitor that the property belonged to another person
options.D: Only where the will contains an express election clause directing the beneficiary to choose

questions.explanation: The doctrine of election is triggered when, on the proper construction of the will, the testator has purported to dispose of property belonging to another while conferring a benefit on that other person. It is not required that the testator expressly acknowledged the property belonged to another, or that they were specifically advised, or that there is an express election clause. The doctrine operates as a consequence of the inconsistency created by the will itself — it is the objective content of the instrument that triggers election, not the testator's subjective intention or knowledge.

questions.question: How does the doctrine of election relate to the broader equitable principle that 'he who seeks equity must do equity'?

options.A: There is no connection between the doctrine of election and this maxim
options.B: The doctrine of election is an application of this maxim — a person who seeks to take a benefit (the equitable benefit under the will) must also do equity (accept the burden imposed by the same will)
options.C: The maxim applies only to plaintiffs seeking injunctions and is irrelevant to election in wills
options.D: The doctrine of election reverses this maxim — equity does equity for the elector without requiring any corresponding obligation

questions.explanation: The doctrine of election is a direct application of the maxim 'he who seeks equity must do equity.' A beneficiary who seeks to take the benefit conferred by the will (seeking equity's assistance to enforce the testamentary gift) must also do equity — that is, they must accept the corresponding obligation imposed by the same instrument. It would be unconscionable for a person to take advantage of part of a will while rejecting another part that imposes a burden on them. The doctrine therefore ensures that the equitable benefit is only available to those who are willing to honour the equitable obligation.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about the doctrine of election is INCORRECT?

options.A: The doctrine requires the person to choose between accepting a benefit under an instrument and giving up their own property as directed, or keeping their own property and forgoing the benefit
options.B: The doctrine is given a statutory basis in Ireland by Section 55 of the Succession Act 1965 in the context of wills
options.C: The doctrine can only arise where the testator deliberately and fraudulently included another person's property in the will
options.D: Where the elector does not make an express election, they are presumed to have elected to take the benefit under the will

questions.explanation: Option C is incorrect. The doctrine of election does not require fraud or deliberate misconduct by the testator. It arises whenever a will purports to dispose of property belonging to another while also conferring a benefit on that person — regardless of whether the testator did so knowingly, innocently, or by mistake. As established in Cooper v Cooper [1874], the doctrine is concerned with the position of the beneficiary (the elector) and not with the testator's state of mind. Options A (correct — the choice available), B (correct — statutory basis in Ireland), and D (correct — the presumption in favour of conforming) are all accurate statements.
